stories.title: Global child deaths fall below 5 million for the first time
stories.summary: UN data shows the under-five mortality rate has more than halved since 2000, from 76 per 1,000 live births to 37. For the first time in recorded history, fewer than 5 million children died before their fifth birthday in a single year — a staggering structural win for humanity.

stories.title: 1.5 billion people have left extreme poverty in 35 years
stories.summary: Our World in Data chronicles one of the quietest triumphs in human history: since 1990, the number of people in extreme poverty has fallen by 1.5 billion — an average of 115,000 people escaping destitution every single day.

stories.title: Solar and wind overtake fossil fuels in the EU for the first time
stories.summary: In 2025, solar and wind produced more electricity than fossil fuels across the European Union — a milestone that would have been unthinkable a decade ago, when fossil fuels generated nearly three times more power than wind and solar combined.

stories.title: Guinea worm disease is down to just 10 cases worldwide
stories.summary: A parasite that once infected 3.5 million people a year is on the verge of becoming only the second human disease ever eradicated, after smallpox — and the first without a drug or vaccine. Eradication would be a civilizational first: extinction by community organizing alone.

stories.title: Malaria vaccines finally reach children across 17 African countries
stories.summary: After decades of failed attempts, the RTS,S and R21 malaria vaccines are now being rolled out to children in 17 endemic countries, including 14 that introduced them for the first time in 2024. Malaria kills a child nearly every minute; this is the beginning of the end of that statistic.

stories.title: 2025 was the year of disease elimination — 17 countries hit the milestone
stories.summary: More countries eliminated a disease in 2025 than in any previous year in history, according to Fix The News's annual progress round-up. Egypt eliminated trachoma; Maldives achieved 'triple elimination' of mother-to-child HIV, syphilis and hepatitis B; Brazil eliminated mother-to-child HIV transmission.

stories.title: India's tiger population has doubled in a decade
stories.summary: The number of wild tigers in India has grown from around 1,700 in 2010 to over 3,600 today — about 75% of the world's tiger population — in one of the largest mammal conservation successes on record, despite India being the world's most populous country.

stories.title: Europe crosses a clean-power tipping point
stories.summary: For the first time, homegrown clean power generated more electricity than fossil fuels in the EU last year — a moment experts describe as a major 'tipping point' for the continent's energy system and a template for the rest of the world.

stories.title: Heart disease deaths fall so fast they drag down global mortality
stories.summary: An Imperial College London analysis found that declining cardiovascular mortality — falling death rates from heart attacks and strokes — was the single biggest driver of the global decline in overall deaths. It's one of the most consequential public-health stories almost no one is reporting.

stories.title: An English farm shows rewilding can bring birds back 10-fold
stories.summary: A 20-year rewilding experiment on a 'dysfunctional' English farm has produced a 900% increase in breeding birds, providing some of the strongest scientific evidence yet that giving land back to nature delivers measurable ecological recovery at scale.

stories.title: The world's first rewilded sharks are thriving
stories.summary: A conservation project in Indonesia has shipped 132 zebra shark eggs, hatched 99 pups and released 43 — an IUCN-modeled pace that could reestablish a functionally extinct population. It's the first time sharks have ever been successfully rewilded, opening a new chapter for ocean conservation.

stories.title: New York City just recorded its fewest shootings in history
stories.summary: The first nine months of 2025 saw fewer shooting incidents in New York City than any period on record. It's part of a broader American story: U.S. gun violence, motor vehicle theft and property crime are all falling at rates unseen in decades.

stories.title: Heat pumps became America's fastest-growing home energy system
stories.summary: Once confined to mild climates, heat pumps now outsell gas furnaces in the United States — a structural shift in how homes are heated that dramatically cuts both emissions and household energy bills. The technology is finally winning on cost and performance.

stories.title: Maternal deaths have dropped 41% worldwide since 2000
stories.summary: A WHO-backed analysis finds that expanded maternity care and family planning have cut global maternal mortality by 41% in just over two decades. Uzbekistan alone has seen maternal deaths fall by nearly a factor of five since 1990.

stories.title: Extreme poverty rate keeps falling, even through the pandemic hangover
stories.summary: World Bank nowcasts show the global extreme poverty rate declined again between 2022 and 2025, despite pandemic shocks, war and inflation. Humanity's long march out of destitution has resumed.

stories.title: Wind and solar just beat coal in the US for the first time ever
stories.summary: U.S. wind and solar combined produced more electricity than coal in 2024, a historic crossover driven by the country adding an estimated 36.4 gigawatts of utility-scale solar in a single year — nearly double the previous record.

stories.title: Australia now protects more ocean than any country on Earth
stories.summary: Australia's expanded marine park network now safeguards more of the sea than any other nation, putting it well beyond the global '30 by 30' goal of protecting 30% of the oceans by 2030 — a conservation benchmark most countries are still chasing.

stories.title: Spain adds six new marine biodiversity hotspots to its protected waters
stories.summary: Spain has designated six new marine protected areas totaling roughly 17,000 square kilometers, expanding its network of conservation zones ahead of the 2030 global ocean-protection deadline and safeguarding some of the Mediterranean's most biodiverse habitats.

stories.title: Scientists grow living human brain tissue to accelerate an Alzheimer's cure
stories.summary: Researchers are using living, donated human brain tissue to model Alzheimer's in the lab — a breakthrough method that could compress the timeline for finding effective therapies for the 153 million people expected to be affected by the disease by 2050.

stories.title: Brazil's Amazon deforestation is on track for the lowest rate on record
stories.summary: Deforestation alerts in the Brazilian Amazon have halved in recent years and are now on pace to hit their lowest level since satellite monitoring began, as enforcement crackdowns on illegal cattle ranching bite. The world's most important rainforest may finally be turning a corner.
